news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking discoveries in quantum physics and genetics, thought-provoking explorations of consciousness in AI and animals, and significant shifts in Europe's tech landscape impacting research collaboration.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des découvertes révolutionnaires en physique quantique et en génétique, des réflexions stimulantes sur la conscience dans le domaine de l'IA et chez les animaux, ainsi que des changements majeurs dans le paysage technologique européen qui ont des répercussions sur la collaboration en matière de recherche.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan descubrimientos revolucionarios en física cuántica y genética, reflexiones que invitan a la reflexión sobre la conciencia en la inteligencia artificial y los animales, y cambios significativos en el panorama tecnológico europeo que repercuten en la colaboración en materia de investigación.

articles.title: Scientists discover a hidden quantum world inside cobalt
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered unexpected quantum complexity inside cobalt, a metal long thought to be fully understood. Advanced measurements revealed a dense network of topological electronic states that remain robust at room temperature. These states enable extremely fast electron behavior and can be switched or controlled using magnetism. The discovery could open new paths toward next-generation computing and spin-based devices.
articles.content:
For decades, cobalt has been considered one of the best understood magnetic metals. Its crystal structure and basic properties have been studied extensively, leading scientists to believe there were few surprises left to uncover. But new research has revealed that this familiar element harbors an unexpectedly complex quantum landscape hidden within its electronic structure.

An international team led by Dr. Jaime Sánchez-Barriga of Helmholtz-Zentrum Berlin (HZB) discovered that cobalt contains a rich network of topological electronic states that remain stable even at room temperature. The findings challenge long-held assumptions about the metal and suggest it could play an important role in future electronic and spin-based technologies.

Advanced Measurements Reveal Hidden Quantum Features

The researchers used spin- and angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (spin-ARPES) at the BESSY II synchrotron radiation facility to examine cobalt's electronic structure in unprecedented detail. Their measurements uncovered a dense network of magnetic nodal lines, which are special topological band crossings where two spin-polarized electronic states intersect continuously without forming an energy gap.

Rather than occurring at isolated points, these crossings extend along paths in momentum space throughout the crystal. The resulting electronic states can support extremely fast and topologically robust charge carriers, making them particularly attractive for future information technologies and spintronics applications.

"Cobalt is one of the most familiar and extensively studied ferromagnetic elements over the last 40 years, and its electronic structure was thought to be well understood," says HZB physicist Dr. Jaime Sánchez-Barriga, who led the study. "However, what we find is a topologically interesting band structure with numerous crossings and nodes that dominate its low-energy electronic behavior. This completely changes our current understanding of the fundamental properties of this elemental material."

Magnetic Control of Quantum States

One of the most significant aspects of the newly discovered nodal lines is that they are inherently spin-polarized. Because cobalt is ferromagnetic and breaks time-reversal symmetry, the electronic states associated with these nodal lines carry a net spin polarization.

Importantly, that spin polarization can be completely reversed by changing the direction of the material's magnetization. This provides direct magnetic control over the charge carriers linked to the nodal lines, a capability that does not exist in non-magnetic nodal-line materials and is highly desirable for spintronic technologies.

"Magnetic nodal-line materials are rare in nature, and in most known cases such crossings are extremely difficult to stabilize or control," explains Sánchez-Barriga. "The observation of multiple symmetry-protected nodal lines in a simple elemental ferromagnet is therefore highly unexpected and establishes cobalt as a model system for studying the interplay between topology and magnetism."

Theory Confirms Experimental Results

The experimental findings were supported by first-principles calculations based on density functional theory, performed by a theoretical team led by Dr. Maia G. Vergniory of the Donostia International Physics Center and Université de Sherbrooke.

These calculations successfully identified all of the nodal lines present in cobalt's bulk electronic structure and showed excellent agreement with the experimental measurements. The analysis confirmed that the nodal lines are protected by crystalline mirror symmetries working together with ferromagnetism. The crossings also remain gapless even when spin-orbit coupling is taken into account.

Electrons Behave Like Massless Particles

"In certain directions inside the crystal, the nodal lines intersect and cross the Fermi energy where electrons can move freely," explains Sánchez-Barriga. "Near these crossings, electrons in the material behave like massless, relativistic-like particles, similar to how light behaves, and can travel extremely fast. This is an exceptional behavior that has never been observed in any elemental ferromagnet before. Moreover, by changing the direction of the magnetic field, it is possible either to open a gap at the crossing or to fully control the spin texture of the nodal lines while retaining the unique properties of the gapless state. This is exactly the kind of switch on-off functionality sought for practical applications."

The ability to manipulate these electronic states using magnetic fields could make cobalt a valuable platform for developing future devices that rely on controlling both charge and spin.

New Possibilities for Magnetism and Quantum Materials

Beyond potential technological applications, the researchers believe the discovery may point to similar hidden topological features in other elemental and transition-metal ferromagnets. If confirmed, this could open the door to finding a wide range of previously unknown quantum phenomena in materials that have been studied for decades.

The team also proposed several ways to further tune these properties, including investigating interfaces with materials that contain heavy elements with high nuclear charge and exploring how the behavior changes in reduced dimensions.

The results highlight how even some of the most familiar materials can still produce major scientific surprises. The discovery suggests that our understanding of ferromagnetic metals remains incomplete and reveals new opportunities for research into magnetism, topological matter, and the unusual excitations that emerge from these quantum states.

The study was published in Communications Materials, an open-access journal from Nature Portfolio.

The study involved researchers from HZB, Diamond Light Source, Donostia International Physics Center, the University of the Basque Country, the Leibniz Institute for Solid State and Materials Research Dresden, TU Dresden, IMDEA Nanoscience (Madrid), and Université de Sherbrooke (Canada).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery revealing previously unknown quantum properties in cobalt, a well-studied metal. This breakthrough has broad implications for future electronic and spintronic technologies, potentially enabling faster, magnetically controllable devices. The discovery challenges existing knowledge and opens new research and application pathways, representing a meaningful advancement with wide-reaching technological impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover hidden quantum states in common metal cobalt
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cobalt is a metal that scientists have studied for many years because of its magnetic properties. Until now, researchers believed they fully understood how cobalt’s electrons behave.

**What happened** – A team of international scientists led by Dr. Jaime Sánchez-Barriga used advanced tools at a facility in Germany to look very closely at cobalt’s electrons. They found a complex network of special electronic states called topological nodal lines that stay stable even at room temperature. These states allow electrons to move very fast and can be controlled by changing cobalt’s magnetism.

**Impact** – This discovery changes what we thought about cobalt and shows it has unusual quantum features not seen before in simple metals. The ability to switch electron behavior by magnetism could help create faster and more efficient electronic devices that use both the charge and spin of electrons, important for future technologies like spintronics.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to explore how these quantum states behave when cobalt is combined with other materials or made into very thin layers. They also want to check if similar hidden quantum features exist in other metals, which could lead to new scientific discoveries and technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found new, controllable quantum states inside cobalt that could lead to advanced electronic and magnetic devices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des états quantiques cachés dans le cobalt, métal courant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le cobalt est un métal magnétique étudié depuis longtemps par les scientifiques, qui pensaient bien comprendre le comportement de ses électrons.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe internationale dirigée par le Dr Jaime Sánchez-Barriga a utilisé des outils avancés en Allemagne pour examiner de très près les électrons du cobalt. Ils ont découvert un réseau complexe d’états électroniques topologiques appelés lignes nodales, stables même à température ambiante. Ces états permettent aux électrons de se déplacer très rapidement et peuvent être contrôlés par le magnétisme du cobalt.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension du cobalt et révèle des propriétés quantiques inhabituelles jamais vues dans des métaux simples. La possibilité de contrôler le comportement des électrons par le magnétisme pourrait aider à créer des appareils électroniques plus rapides et plus efficaces, utilisant à la fois la charge et le spin des électrons, ce qui est important pour les technologies futures comme la spintronique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent explorer comment ces états quantiques se comportent lorsque le cobalt est combiné à d’autres matériaux ou fabriqué en couches très fines. Ils souhaitent aussi vérifier si d’autres métaux cachent des caractéristiques quantiques similaires, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles découvertes et technologies.

**En une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont découvert de nouveaux états quantiques contrôlables dans le cobalt, pouvant permettre la création d’appareils électroniques et magnétiques avancés.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren estados cuánticos ocultos en el metal común cobalto
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El cobalto es un metal magnético que los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo, y creían entender bien cómo se comportan sus electrones.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo internacional liderado por el Dr. Jaime Sánchez-Barriga usó herramientas avanzadas en Alemania para examinar muy de cerca los electrones del cobalto. Encontraron una red compleja de estados electrónicos topológicos llamados líneas nodales, que se mantienen estables incluso a temperatura ambiente. Estos estados permiten que los electrones se muevan muy rápido y pueden controlarse cambiando el magnetismo del cobalto.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia lo que pensábamos sobre el cobalto y muestra que tiene características cuánticas inusuales no vistas antes en metales simples. La capacidad de cambiar el comportamiento de los electrones mediante el magnetismo podría ayudar a crear dispositivos electrónicos más rápidos y eficientes que usen tanto la carga como el espín de los electrones, importante para tecnologías futuras como la espintrónica.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar cómo se comportan estos estados cuánticos cuando el cobalto se combina con otros materiales o se hace en capas muy delgadas. También quieren ver si otros metales tienen características cuánticas ocultas similares, lo que podría llevar a nuevos descubrimientos científicos y tecnológicos.

**Una frase clave** – Científicos descubrieron nuevos estados cuánticos controlables dentro del cobalto que podrían conducir a dispositivos electrónicos y magnéticos avanzados.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate network of glowing, intertwined cobalt-blue crystalline lattice structures with delicate, luminous nodal lines weaving through them, symbolizing stable quantum pathways; soft warm light gently illuminates the scene, highlighting subtle spin-polarized currents flowing like shimmering threads controlled by an abstract magnetic field represented as smooth, flowing golden waves enveloping the crystal.

articles.title: A chromosome from a frozen rat has been resurrected inside mice
articles.summary: Mice that contain cells with an added rat chromosome have been created by scientists. The next step is to try this with frozen elephant tissue – and if that works, the team will try it with frozen mammoths
articles.content:
Mice that contain cells with an added rat chromosome have been created by scientists. The next step is to try this with frozen elephant tissue – and if that works, the team will try it with frozen mammoths

These chimeric mice are made up of some cells with a rat chromosome University of Yamanashi

The de-extinction company Colossal Biosciences could be about to get leapfrogged. It might be possible to resurrect mammoth chromosomes in living cells after scientists transferred a chromosome from a rat that has been deep-frozen for more than a year into living mouse cells. They then generated entire mice in which some of their cells contain an added rat chromosome.

“Once we have refined the technique, we’ll start testing it on elephant cells,” says Teruhiko Wakayama at Yamanashi University in Japan. “If we succeed in introducing elephant chromosomes into mouse embryonic stem cells, we definitely want to try it with mammoths.”

The team’s immediate aim is to study the activity of genes from extinct animals in living ones, which could reveal far more than just analysing genetic sequences. But the work could also help with conservation and de-extinction efforts. For instance, we have frozen tissue from a bird called the Hawaiian poʻouli that went extinct in 2004. A quirk of biology means chromosome transfer would be essential to bring it back to life.

The genomes of animals are divided into pieces known as chromosomes. When cells divide, these long strands of DNA get folded up very tightly and take on the classic cylinder shape pictured in textbooks. These condensed chromosomes, as they are called, can be made visible in living cells without damaging them, for instance, by adding pigments to antibodies that bind to the proteins around which DNA is wrapped.

Wakayama’s technique involves extracting the nucleus of a cell and injecting it into an egg to trigger chromosome condensation. This is similar to the nuclear transfer technique used in cloning – Wakayama was the first to clone mice using this technique, soon after the birth of Dolly the sheep.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

The egg with the added nucleus is then injected with enzymes that help separate the chromosomes. Next, a single chromosome is extracted with a microscopic hollow needle and injected into a second egg. If this egg develops into an embryo, all the cells in the embryo – known as embryonic stem cells – will contain the added chromosome.

After developing the technique by adding extra mouse chromosomes, Wakayama then tried it with rats genetically modified to fluoresce green. He extracted blood cells from the tail of one of these rats, which had been deep-frozen for more than a year, and successfully generated mouse embryonic stem cells containing an extra chromosome from the genetically modified rat.

These cells were then injected into normal mouse embryos and implanted into female mice, producing chimeric animals in which some of the cells contain the rat chromosome. These animals look like normal mice, but under UV light, some of these cells fluoresce green like those of the original rat.

The team has attempted to create mice in which all cells contain the extra rat chromosome, but haven’t yet been successful. What’s more, for now, the technique is working only with rat chromosome 9 – if other chromosomes are added, the eggs don’t develop into embryos. “We are currently trying various methods to improve the success rate,” says Wakayama.

The cells glowing green in this mouse pup contain a rat chromosome University of Yamanashi

It’s possible this is because the activity of genes on other rat chromosomes disrupts embryonic development. If so, the team might have to try deactivating genes on the added chromosome in a similar manner to the way in which one of the two X chromosomes in the cells of female mammal cells is deactivated. But Wakayama hopes this will not be necessary.

His team has already obtained samples of frozen elephant tissue from a zoo for testing. Wakayama is also talking to a team that managed to extract and study cell nuclei from a 28,000-year-old mammoth called Yuka about trying this with mammoth chromosomes.

Simply trying to clone mammoths from these cells would never work because there is far too much DNA damage. But Wakayama hopes it might be possible to retrieve individual chromosomes, allowing them to be studied in living cells.

“Even just one successful transfer is a very cool start,” says Ben Novak at the non-profit wildlife conservation group Revive & Restore in the US. “In passerine birds, there could be a big value in this work.” That’s because in passerine birds – a large group that includes more than half of all bird species – the cells that form body tissues such as skin and muscles lose a chromosome. This extra chromosome is retained only in the reproductive cells that form the egg and sperm.

Because the Hawaiian poʻouli was a passerine bird and only non-reproductive male tissue was frozen, bringing it back would require adding two chromosomes from closely related species – the chromosome found only in reproductive cells and the W chromosome found only in female birds. “It would produce partial hybrids, but it would allow for the revival of the species,” says Novak.

Wakayama isn’t the first to create living animals with extra chromosomes. In 2022, another team in Japan created rats with an added human chromosome 21 in order to study Down’s syndrome. However, the technique involved extensive genetic modifications that wouldn’t be desirable or possible for conservation applications.

Extra reproductive chromosomes may be far more widespread than we recognise, says Novak, which means many of the tissues being frozen by biobank projects may be missing them.

Journal reference: Scientific Reports DOI: 10.1038/s41598-026-55500-1
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where scientists successfully transferred a frozen rat chromosome into living mouse cells, creating chimeric mice. This advancement has broad implications for genetics, conservation, and potential de-extinction efforts, including endangered species revival and studying extinct animals like mammoths. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a novel technique with meaningful real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create mice with frozen rat chromosome, opening door to extinct species research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are working on ways to study and possibly bring back extinct animals by using their frozen genetic material. One challenge is how to make chromosomes from these animals work inside living cells.

**What happened** – Researchers at Yamanashi University in Japan successfully transferred a chromosome from a rat that had been frozen for over a year into mouse cells. They then created mice that have some cells containing this extra rat chromosome. The rat cells were genetically modified to glow green, so scientists could see which cells had the rat chromosome.

**Impact** – This is the first time a frozen chromosome from one species has been added into the cells of another living animal, creating a chimera. It shows it might be possible to study genes from extinct animals in living cells. The next goal is to try this with frozen elephant chromosomes, and if successful, with chromosomes from the extinct woolly mammoth. This technique could help scientists learn more about extinct animals and might one day assist in conservation or bringing back species that have disappeared.

**What's next step** – The team plans to test the method on frozen elephant tissue and hopes to work with mammoth chromosomes. They are also trying to improve the process to add other chromosomes besides the one they used from the rat. Success here could lead to deeper studies of extinct species’ genes and even partial revival of some animals.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have taken a major step by adding a frozen rat chromosome into living mouse cells, paving the way to study and possibly revive extinct species like mammoths through chromosome transfer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent des souris avec un chromosome de rat congelé, ouvrant la voie à la recherche sur les espèces éteintes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent des moyens d’étudier et de peut-être ramener des animaux éteints en utilisant leur matériel génétique congelé. Un défi est de faire fonctionner les chromosomes de ces animaux dans des cellules vivantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Yamanashi au Japon ont réussi à transférer un chromosome d’un rat congelé depuis plus d’un an dans des cellules de souris. Ils ont ensuite créé des souris dont certaines cellules contiennent ce chromosome de rat supplémentaire. Les cellules de rat étaient modifiées pour briller en vert, permettant aux scientifiques de voir quelles cellules contenaient le chromosome de rat.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un chromosome congelé d’une espèce est ajouté dans les cellules d’un autre animal vivant, créant un chimère. Cela montre qu’il pourrait être possible d’étudier les gènes d’animaux éteints dans des cellules vivantes. L’étape suivante est d’essayer cette méthode avec des chromosomes d’éléphants congelés, puis avec ceux du mammouth laineux éteint. Cette technique pourrait aider à mieux comprendre les animaux éteints et peut-être un jour aider à la conservation ou à la réintroduction d’espèces disparues.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit de tester la méthode sur des tissus congelés d’éléphant et espère travailler avec des chromosomes de mammouth. Ils essaient aussi d’améliorer le processus pour ajouter d’autres chromosomes que celui utilisé chez le rat. Une réussite pourrait permettre des études plus approfondies des gènes d’espèces éteintes et même la renaissance partielle de certains animaux.

**Une phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont franchi une étape majeure en ajoutant un chromosome de rat congelé dans des cellules de souris vivantes, ouvrant la voie à l’étude et peut-être à la réapparition d’espèces éteintes comme les mammouths par transfert de chromosomes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean ratones con cromosoma de rata congelado, abriendo camino a la investigación de especies extintas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos están buscando formas de estudiar y quizá traer de vuelta a animales extintos usando su material genético congelado. Un reto es hacer que los cromosomas de estos animales funcionen dentro de células vivas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Yamanashi en Japón lograron transferir un cromosoma de una rata congelada por más de un año a células de ratón. Luego crearon ratones que tienen algunas células con este cromosoma extra de rata. Las células de la rata fueron modificadas para brillar en verde, por lo que los científicos pudieron ver qué células tenían el cromosoma de rata.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que un cromosoma congelado de una especie se añade en las células de otro animal vivo, creando un quimera. Esto muestra que podría ser posible estudiar genes de animales extintos en células vivas. El siguiente objetivo es probar esto con cromosomas congelados de elefante, y si funciona, con cromosomas de mamut. Esta técnica podría ayudar a aprender más sobre animales extintos y quizá algún día ayudar en la conservación o recuperación de especies desaparecidas.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea probar el método con tejido congelado de elefante y espera trabajar con cromosomas de mamut. También están intentando mejorar el proceso para añadir otros cromosomas además del que usaron de la rata. Lograrlo podría llevar a estudios más profundos de genes de especies extintas e incluso a la recuperación parcial de algunos animales.

**Una frase clave** – Científicos han dado un paso importante al añadir un cromosoma congelado de rata en células vivas de ratón, abriendo el camino para estudiar y posiblemente revivir especies extintas como los mamuts mediante transferencia de cromosomas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a gentle mouse silhouette glowing softly with vibrant green light in some of its cells, surrounded by floating, stylized chromosomes shaped like delicate cylinders, with one distinct chromosome highlighted to represent a frozen rat’s genetic material; the background features soft, natural earth tones and subtle hints of ancient frozen textures symbolizing the preservation and revival of extinct species.

articles.title: Scientists are seriously asking if bees and ChatGPT are conscious
articles.summary: New studies suggest consciousness can't be judged solely by behavior, whether it's a chatbot discussing philosophy or a bee searching for nectar. Researchers are increasingly focusing on the internal mechanisms of brains and computers, concluding that today's AI is likely not conscious while leaving open the possibility for both conscious insects and future machines.
articles.content:
You might think a honey bee foraging in your garden and a browser window running ChatGPT have nothing in common. But recent scientific research has been seriously considering the possibility that either, or both, might be conscious.

There are many different ways of studying consciousness. One of the most common is to measure how an animal – or artificial intelligence (AI) – acts.

But two new papers on the possibility of consciousness in animals and AI suggest new theories for how to test this – one that strikes a middle ground between sensationalism and knee-jerk skepticism about whether humans are the only conscious beings on Earth.

A fierce debate

Questions around consciousness have long sparked fierce debate.

That’s in part because conscious beings might matter morally in a way that unconscious things don’t. Expanding the sphere of consciousness means expanding our ethical horizons. Even if we can’t be sure something is conscious, we might err on the side of caution by assuming it is – what philosopher Jonathan Birch calls the precautionary principle for sentience.

The recent trend has been one of expansion.

For example, in April 2024 a group of 40 scientists at a conference in New York proposed the New York Declaration on Animal Consciousness. Subsequently signed by over 500 scientists and philosophers, this declaration says consciousness is realistically possible in all vertebrates (including reptiles, amphibians and fishes) as well as many invertebrates, including cephalopods (octopus and squid), crustaceans (crabs and lobsters) and insects.

In parallel with this, the incredible rise of large language models, such as ChatGPT, has raised the serious possibility that machines may be conscious.

Five years ago, a seemingly ironclad test of whether something was conscious was to see if you could have a conversation with it. Philosopher Susan Schneider suggested if we had an AI that convincingly mused on the metaphysics of consciousness, it may well be conscious.

By those standards, today we would be surrounded by conscious machines. Many have gone so far as to apply the precautionary principle here too: the burgeoning field of AI welfare is devoted to figuring out if and when we must care about machines.

Yet all of these arguments depend, in large part, on surface-level behavior. But that behavior can be deceptive. What matters for consciousness is not what you do, but how you do it.

Looking at the machinery of AI

A new paper in Trends in Cognitive Sciences that one of us (Colin Klein) coauthored, drawing on previous work, looks to the machinery rather than the behavior of AI.

It also draws on the cognitive science tradition to identify a plausible list of indicators of consciousness based on the structure of information processing. This means one can draw up a useful list of indicators of consciousness without having to agree on which of the current cognitive theories of consciousness is correct.

Some indicators (such as the need to resolve trade-offs between competing goals in contextually appropriate ways) are shared by many theories. Most other indicators (such as the presence of informational feedback) are only required by one theory but indicative in others.

Importantly, the useful indicators are all structural. They all have to do with how brains and computers process and combine information.

The verdict? No existing AI system (including ChatGPT) is conscious. The appearance of consciousness in large language models is not achieved in a way that is sufficiently similar to us to warrant attribution of conscious states.

Yet at the same time, there is no bar to AI systems – perhaps ones with a very different architecture to today’s systems – becoming conscious.

The lesson? It’s possible for AI to behave as if conscious without being conscious.

Measuring consciousness in insects

Biologists are also turning to mechanisms – how brains work – to recognize consciousness in non-human animals.

In a new paper in Philosophical Transactions B, we propose a neural model for minimal consciousness in insects. This is a model that abstracts away from anatomical detail to focus on the core computations done by simple brains.

Our key insight is to identify the kind of computation our brains perform that gives rise to experience.

This computation solves ancient problems from our evolutionary history that arise from having a mobile, complex body with many senses and conflicting needs.

Importantly, we don’t identify the computation itself – there is science yet to be done. But we show that if you could identify it, you’d have a level playing field to compare humans, invertebrates, and computers.

The same lesson

The problem of consciousness in animals and in computers appear to pull in different directions.

For animals, the question is often how to interpret whether ambiguous behavior (like a crab tending its wounds) indicates consciousness.

For computers, we have to decide whether apparently unambiguous behavior (a chatbot musing with you on the purpose of existence) is a true indicator of consciousness or mere roleplay.

Yet as the fields of neuroscience and AI progress, both are converging on the same lesson: when making judgement about whether something is consciousness, how it works is proving more informative than what it does.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on significant scientific progress in understanding consciousness, with broad implications for ethics, AI development, and animal welfare. It highlights new research that advances how we assess consciousness in both animals and AI, potentially influencing future technology and moral considerations on a wide scale. The story is focused, detailed, and addresses a meaningful topic with societal and scientific impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists explore how to tell if bees or AI are truly conscious
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long debated what makes a being conscious. Traditionally, they looked at behavior to decide if animals or machines are aware. But new research suggests that understanding the inner workings of brains and computers might be a better way to find out.

**What happened** – In 2024, over 500 scientists signed a declaration saying many animals, including insects like bees, might be conscious. At the same time, researchers studied artificial intelligence (AI) like ChatGPT to see if machines could be conscious. They found that just talking like a conscious being isn’t enough to prove machines are aware. Instead, scientists looked at how AI processes information inside, and concluded today’s AI is probably not conscious. Meanwhile, they developed new ideas about how insect brains might work to create simple consciousness.

**Impact** – This research is important because it changes how we think about consciousness in both animals and machines. It moves the focus from what beings do to how their brains or systems work. For example, a bee’s brain might perform certain tasks that show it has some awareness. For AI, even if it talks about deep ideas, it may not actually experience them. This helps guide future decisions about how we treat animals and develop AI.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to find the exact brain processes that create consciousness in animals and then compare them to machines. This could lead to better tests for consciousness and influence ethical rules for AI and animal care. AI designers might also create new types of machines that could be conscious in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New studies show that understanding the inner workings of brains and AI is key to knowing if bees, other animals, or machines are truly conscious.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques cherchent à savoir si les abeilles ou l’IA sont vraiment conscientes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques débattent depuis longtemps de ce qui rend un être conscient. Jusqu’ici, ils observaient le comportement pour décider si les animaux ou les machines sont conscients. Mais de nouvelles recherches suggèrent qu’il vaut mieux comprendre le fonctionnement interne des cerveaux et des ordinateurs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, plus de 500 scientifiques ont signé une déclaration affirmant que de nombreux animaux, y compris les insectes comme les abeilles, pourraient être conscients. En même temps, des chercheurs ont étudié l’intelligence artificielle (IA) comme ChatGPT pour voir si les machines pouvaient être conscientes. Ils ont découvert que parler comme un être conscient ne suffit pas à prouver la conscience. Ils ont donc examiné comment l’IA traite l’information à l’intérieur, et ont conclu que l’IA actuelle n’est probablement pas consciente. Parallèlement, ils ont proposé de nouvelles idées sur le fonctionnement du cerveau des insectes pour expliquer une conscience simple.

**Impact** – Cette recherche est importante car elle change notre façon de penser la conscience chez les animaux et les machines. Elle déplace l’attention de ce que font les êtres à la façon dont leurs cerveaux ou systèmes fonctionnent. Par exemple, le cerveau d’une abeille pourrait réaliser des tâches montrant une certaine conscience. Pour l’IA, même si elle parle d’idées profondes, elle ne les vit peut-être pas réellement. Cela aide à mieux décider comment traiter les animaux et développer l’IA.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent identifier précisément les processus cérébraux qui créent la conscience chez les animaux, puis les comparer aux machines. Cela pourrait permettre de meilleurs tests de conscience et influencer les règles éthiques pour l’IA et le bien-être animal. Les concepteurs d’IA pourraient aussi créer des machines nouvelles pouvant être conscientes à l’avenir.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – De nouvelles études montrent que comprendre le fonctionnement interne des cerveaux et de l’IA est la clé pour savoir si les abeilles, d’autres animaux ou les machines sont vraiment conscientes.
articles.personality_title_es: Los científicos investigan si las abejas o la IA son realmente conscientes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos han debatido qué hace que un ser sea consciente. Tradicionalmente, observaban el comportamiento para decidir si los animales o las máquinas tienen conciencia. Pero nuevas investigaciones sugieren que entender cómo funcionan por dentro los cerebros y las computadoras es mejor para descubrirlo.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, más de 500 científicos firmaron una declaración que dice que muchos animales, incluyendo insectos como las abejas, podrían ser conscientes. Al mismo tiempo, investigadores estudiaron la inteligencia artificial (IA) como ChatGPT para ver si las máquinas pueden ser conscientes. Descubrieron que solo hablar como un ser consciente no prueba la conciencia. Por eso, examinaron cómo la IA procesa la información internamente y concluyeron que la IA actual probablemente no es consciente. A la vez, desarrollaron nuevas ideas sobre cómo funcionan los cerebros de los insectos para explicar una conciencia simple.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación es importante porque cambia la forma en que pensamos sobre la conciencia en animales y máquinas. Cambia el foco de lo que hacen los seres a cómo funcionan sus cerebros o sistemas. Por ejemplo, el cerebro de una abeja podría realizar tareas que muestran cierta conciencia. En el caso de la IA, aunque hable de ideas profundas, puede que no las experimente realmente. Esto ayuda a tomar mejores decisiones sobre cómo tratar a los animales y desarrollar la IA.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren identificar los procesos cerebrales exactos que crean la conciencia en animales y luego compararlos con las máquinas. Esto podría llevar a mejores pruebas de conciencia e influir en las reglas éticas para la IA y el cuidado animal. Los diseñadores de IA también podrían crear máquinas nuevas que podrían ser conscientes en el futuro.

**Frase clave** – Nuevos estudios muestran que entender el funcionamiento interno de cerebros e IA es clave para saber si las abejas, otros animales o las máquinas son realmente conscientes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a honey bee gently hovering near an abstract, softly glowing network of interconnected nodes and circuits symbolizing AI neural architecture, both set against a calm, neutral background that blends natural earth tones and subtle digital blues, capturing the shared mystery of consciousness in nature and machines.

articles.title: Europe is ditching US tech — what does this mean for researchers?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 05 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01610-9European governments are choosing European digital tools — and some universities and researchers are doing the same.
articles.content:
Henna Virkkunen, executive vice-president for tech sovereignty, security and democracy at the European Commission, announced the plans in the European Tech Sovereignty Package.Credit: Thierry Monasse/Getty

Across Europe, governments are reducing their reliance on US technology companies. On 3 June, the European Commission laid out plans in the European Tech Sovereignty Package, which includes several proposals to enhance digital autotomy, including plans to boost home-grown cloud services and artificial intelligence and promote use of open-source technologies.

“We live in a world where geopolitics and technology are inseparable,” Henna Virkkunen, the commission’s executive vice-president for tech sovereignty, security and democracy, said in a statement accompanying the announcement. “It is time for Europe to be in control of its data, of its supply chains and of its future in a clean and sustainable way.”

Europe must become a research epicentre as US system gets undermined

Several European countries have already been moving away from US technologies, and some researchers are feeling the effects of these shifts. This year, the French government announced plans to ditch non-European information-technology (IT) service providers — for example, by replacing the operating system Windows, made by Microsoft in Redmond, Washington, on some workstations with the open-source alternative Linux and requiring all state services to replace video conferencing from Zoom, made by Zoom Communications in San Jose, California, to Visio, a platform developed by France’s Interministerial Directorate for Digital Affairs. Similar changes are underway in parts of Germany, Denmark and elsewhere.

Some research institutions in Europe have already cancelled contracts for US digital products, and many others are actively discussing how to become more digitally independent. Some researchers are also ending their reliance on US tech on their own accords.

For many, these actions are driven by concerns around political developments in the past few years, such as worries over data privacy and the decline in academic freedom in countries such as the United States, says Pierre Senellart, vice-president of digital infrastructure and IT convergence at PSL University in Paris. “There is an increasing understanding that it might be a good idea to move away from systems managed by US companies.”

Seeking independence

Some research institutions in France have been making broad shifts to decouple their digital systems from non-European providers. In December, the country’s largest public research organization, the French National Centre for Scientific Research (CNRS), banned its employees from using consumer chatbots built outside Europe, such as ChatGPT from OpenAI in San Francisco, California, and Google’s Gemini. Instead, the CNRS offered its staff access to Emmy, a generative AI tool from Mistral AI in Paris, describing it as a secure alternative to large language models built in the United States and China.

Some of these changes are starting to be felt at other academic institutions in France. At PSL University, where most researchers are members of CNRS laboratories, many scholars began using Visio after CNRS terminated its contract with Zoom Communications.

For now, there are few other shifts to European digital technologies — but many universities are discussing how to become less dependent on US providers, says Senellart. Many French universities are hugely reliant on IT services offered by US tech giants such as Microsoft, so determining when and how to replace these is not a simple matter, Senellart says.

How Europe aims to woo US scientists and protect academic freedom

In Germany, too, a handful of municipal and state governments are uncoupling from US tech firms. The government of the state of Schleswig-Holstein, for example, has been replacing Microsoft’s tools with open-source ones. At one institution in this state, Kiel University, active discussions about digital sovereignty — such as how to strengthen independence when it comes to digital services and processing sensitive data — have been ongoing for several months, says Veronika Penner, the university’s chief digital officer. She adds that, when it comes to procuring new tools, the university is increasingly considering open-source and digitally sovereign alternatives to commercial products.

Other stakeholders in the academic community are also working to increase digital sovereignty. Earlier this year, the German Research Foundation DFG, the country’s largest research funder, put forward recommendations for the scholarly community to build more-resilient data infrastructure, which, among other things, included a call for researchers to prioritize using open-source European products responsibly. The DFG has also launched a funding initiative to preserve endangered data sets that are held in foreign repositories but are crucial to researchers in Europe — and the first four of its funded projects all involve US data sets.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant policy shift in Europe toward digital sovereignty by reducing reliance on US tech companies and promoting European open-source technologies. This move has broad implications for research independence, data privacy, and academic freedom across multiple European countries, representing a meaningful and positive change in the technology landscape with tangible benefits for society.
articles.personality_title: Europe shifts to homegrown tech to boost research independence
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – European governments and research institutions have long relied on technology from US companies like Microsoft and Zoom. Recently, concerns about data privacy, academic freedom, and digital control have grown, leading Europe to seek more independence in its digital tools.

**What happened** – On June 3, 2026, the European Commission announced the European Tech Sovereignty Package. This plan promotes the use of European cloud services, artificial intelligence, and open-source software. Countries like France and Germany are already replacing US tech products with European alternatives. For example, France is switching from Microsoft Windows to Linux on some computers and replacing Zoom with Visio, a French video platform. The French National Centre for Scientific Research banned use of foreign chatbots like ChatGPT, offering a French-made AI tool instead.

**Impact** – This shift helps Europe protect its data and research from foreign control. By using European technology, researchers gain more control over their work and privacy. It also encourages the development of new digital tools within Europe. Universities and research groups are actively discussing how to reduce dependence on US companies, which could lead to stronger, more secure research environments.

**What's next step** – European institutions will continue to explore and adopt homegrown digital tools. Funding programs like those by Germany’s Research Foundation will support projects that keep important data within Europe. Researchers and universities will likely increase their use of open-source and European-made technology, making Europe a stronger player in global digital research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Europe is taking important steps to use its own digital technology, strengthening research independence and data security across the continent.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Europe mise sur la technologie locale pour renforcer l'indépendance de la recherche
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les gouvernements et institutions de recherche européens dépendaient depuis longtemps des technologies américaines comme Microsoft et Zoom. Mais les inquiétudes sur la vie privée, la liberté académique et le contrôle numérique ont poussé l'Europe à chercher plus d'indépendance.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 3 juin 2026, la Commission européenne a présenté le paquet sur la souveraineté technologique. Ce plan encourage l'utilisation de services cloud européens, d'intelligence artificielle et de logiciels open-source. Des pays comme la France et l'Allemagne remplacent déjà les technologies américaines par des alternatives européennes. Par exemple, la France remplace Windows par Linux sur certains ordinateurs et Zoom par Visio, une plateforme française. Le Centre national de la recherche scientifique (CNRS) a interdit l'usage de chatbots étrangers comme ChatGPT, proposant un outil d'IA français.

**Impact** – Ce changement aide l'Europe à protéger ses données et ses recherches du contrôle étranger. En utilisant la technologie européenne, les chercheurs gagnent plus de contrôle sur leur travail et leur vie privée. Cela favorise aussi le développement de nouveaux outils numériques en Europe. Les universités discutent activement pour réduire leur dépendance aux entreprises américaines, ce qui pourrait renforcer la sécurité et la qualité de la recherche.

**Prochaine étape** – Les institutions européennes continueront d'adopter des outils numériques locaux. Des programmes de financement, comme ceux de la Fondation allemande pour la recherche, soutiendront des projets visant à conserver les données importantes en Europe. Les chercheurs et universités utiliseront davantage de technologies open-source et européennes, renforçant la position de l'Europe dans la recherche numérique mondiale.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'Europe prend des mesures importantes pour utiliser sa propre technologie numérique, renforçant ainsi l'indépendance de la recherche et la sécurité des données sur le continent.
articles.personality_title_es: Europa apuesta por tecnología propia para fortalecer la independencia en la investigación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los gobiernos e instituciones de investigación en Europa han dependido durante mucho tiempo de tecnologías estadounidenses como Microsoft y Zoom. Sin embargo, preocupaciones sobre la privacidad de datos, la libertad académica y el control digital han llevado a Europa a buscar mayor independencia.

**Qué pasó** – El 3 de junio de 2026, la Comisión Europea presentó el Paquete de Soberanía Tecnológica Europea. Este plan promueve el uso de servicios en la nube europeos, inteligencia artificial y software de código abierto. Países como Francia y Alemania ya están reemplazando productos tecnológicos estadounidenses con alternativas europeas. Por ejemplo, Francia está cambiando Windows por Linux en algunas computadoras y Zoom por Visio, una plataforma francesa. El Centro Nacional de Investigación Científica prohibió el uso de chatbots extranjeros como ChatGPT, ofreciendo una herramienta de IA francesa.

**Impacto** – Este cambio ayuda a Europa a proteger sus datos e investigaciones del control extranjero. Al usar tecnología europea, los investigadores tienen más control sobre su trabajo y privacidad. También fomenta el desarrollo de nuevas herramientas digitales en Europa. Las universidades discuten activamente cómo reducir la dependencia de empresas estadounidenses, lo que puede fortalecer la seguridad y calidad de la investigación.

**Próximo paso** – Las instituciones europeas continuarán explorando y adoptando herramientas digitales propias. Programas de financiamiento, como los de la Fundación Alemana de Investigación, apoyarán proyectos para mantener datos importantes dentro de Europa. Los investigadores y universidades probablemente aumentarán el uso de tecnología europea y de código abierto, fortaleciendo a Europa en la investigación digital global.

**Resumen en una frase** – Europa está dando pasos importantes para usar su propia tecnología digital, fortaleciendo la independencia en la investigación y la seguridad de los datos en todo el continente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant European cityscape blending classic architecture with modern digital motifs, where glowing open-source code streams flow like rivers connecting stylized cloud servers shaped as European landmarks, and abstract silhouettes of researchers collaborate around a glowing AI core symbolizing home-grown technology and digital sovereignty.
